title: Calling people and counting
summary: Address people with the vocative case and recognize Czech numbers.
objectives: Form common vocative forms of Czech first names
Use pane and paní when addressing someone
Recognize and produce numbers from zero through one hundred

sections.heading: When Czech uses the vocative
sections.body: Use the vocative form when calling to someone, greeting them directly, or getting their attention.

sections.heading: Names ending in -a and -e
sections.body: Names ending in -a usually change to -o. Women's names ending in -e normally do not change.

sections.heading: Men's names ending in a consonant
sections.body: Add -u after k, g, h, ch; add -i after š, ž, ř, c, j; otherwise add -e. Some names lose a fleeting e. Petr is irregular.

sections.heading: Numbers 0–20
sections.body: Learn the basic number words. Jeden has gendered forms jeden, jedna, jedno; two is dva with masculine nouns and dvě with feminine or neuter nouns.
examples: nula, jeden, dva, tři, čtyři, pět

sections.heading: Larger and compound numbers
sections.body: Czech allows both tens-first forms such as dvacet jedna and combined one-and-twenty forms such as jednadvacet. Both are correct.

resources.title: The vocative case
resources.note: Vocative patterns for names, titles, and irregular forms.

exercises.prompt: You are greeting Klára directly. Which form belongs after Ahoj?
exercises.explanation: Names ending in -a normally use -o in the vocative: Kláro.

exercises.prompt: Type the vocative form of Pavel.
exercises.explanation: Pavel loses its fleeting e and adds -e: Pavle.

exercises.prompt: Type one correct Czech form for 21.
exercises.explanation: Both dvacet jedna and jednadvacet are accepted.
